era_context: The Roman Empire under Constantine I (r. 306–337 CE) is undergoing profound religious and administrative transformation. The Council of Nicaea convenes this year, marking the first ecumenical council and a watershed moment in Christian orthodoxy. Constantine has recently consolidated power after defeating Licinius (324 CE) and is relocating the imperial capital eastward. The empire is administratively divided into Eastern and Western halves, though unified under Constantine's rule.

events.title: First Council of Nicaea
events.description: Constantine I convenes approximately 300 bishops at Nicaea to resolve the Arian controversy—a theological dispute over the nature of Christ and his relationship to God the Father. The council condemns Arianism as heretical and affirms the Nicene Creed, establishing that Christ is of one substance (homoousios) with the Father. This decision becomes foundational to Christian orthodoxy and grants Constantine unprecedented authority over ecclesiastical matters. The council also standardizes Easter calculations and establishes canons governing clerical celibacy and church discipline.
key_figures: Nicholas of Myra (Bishop, later venerated as Saint Nicholas)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine (Vita Constantini)
Athanasius of Alexandria, Against the Arians

events.certainty_note: The council's occurrence and basic proceedings are attested in multiple near-contemporary and later sources. The exact number of bishops (often cited as 318, sometimes 300) varies in sources, but the event itself is historically certain.
cross_references: Led to 381-001 (First Council of Constantinople, which reaffirmed Nicene orthodoxy)

events.title: Constantine's Visit to Rome
events.description: Constantine I visits Rome for the first time as sole emperor, celebrating the 20th anniversary (vicennalia) of his reign. The visit is ceremonial and symbolic, reinforcing his legitimacy as emperor. However, Constantine does not reside in Rome; his attention is focused on the East and the construction of a new capital. The visit marks a subtle shift in the empire's center of gravity away from the traditional capital.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's visit to Rome is attested in sources, though the exact date and duration are debated. Some sources place it in 326 CE. The vicennalia celebration is confirmed, but details of the visit's political significance are partly reconstructed.

events.title: Execution of Crispus, Constantine's Son
events.description: Constantine I orders the execution of his eldest son Crispus, Caesar of the Western Roman Empire, on charges of treason and adultery with his stepmother Fausta. The exact circumstances remain obscure and contested by ancient sources. Some accounts suggest Fausta falsely accused Crispus; others claim genuine conspiracy. The execution shocks the empire and raises questions about Constantine's judgment and the stability of the succession. Shortly after, Constantine also executes Fausta, possibly by scalding her in a bath.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The execution of Crispus is historically certain, but the dating (325 or 326 CE) and the precise charges are disputed. Ancient sources offer conflicting narratives, suggesting the event was politically sensitive and later reinterpreted.
cross_references: Part of broader succession crisis in Constantine's dynasty

events.title: Continued Construction of Constantinople
events.region: Byzantium, Eastern Roman Empire (modern-day Istanbul, Turkey)
events.description: Constantine I continues the massive construction project to transform Byzantium into a new imperial capital, which he will formally dedicate in 330 CE. The city is being rebuilt with new walls, forums, churches, and administrative buildings. This represents an unprecedented investment in urban infrastructure and signals Constantine's intention to shift the empire's political center eastward, away from Rome. The project employs tens of thousands of workers and artisans.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The construction of Constantinople is well-attested in multiple sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence. The timeline of construction phases is debated, but work was ongoing throughout the 320s CE.
cross_references: Part of broader administrative reorganization under Constantine

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Constantine as a pious Christian emperor devoted to religious unity is complicated by several factors: (1) The Council of Nicaea, while presented as Constantine's effort to resolve theological dispute, was also a tool of imperial control over the church—Constantine presided over the council and enforced its decisions through imperial authority, blurring the line between secular and religious power. (2) Constantine's execution of Crispus and Fausta, occurring around the time of the council, suggests that Constantine's piety did not prevent brutal dynastic politics. (3) Eusebius of Caesarea's Life of Constantine, a primary source for this period, is hagiographic and omits or downplays Constantine's violence and political ruthlessness. (4) The Arian controversy did not end with Nicaea; Arianism remained influential in the Eastern Empire and among Germanic peoples for centuries, suggesting the council's 'victory' was less complete than traditional accounts imply.
historiographic_note: The year 325 CE is exceptionally well-documented for the Roman Empire, primarily through ecclesiastical sources (Eusebius, Socrates, later church historians) and imperial records. However, these sources have significant biases: ecclesiastical sources emphasize religious significance and Constantine's role as a Christian emperor, while downplaying political violence. Imperial records are fragmentary. Non-Christian perspectives on Constantine's reign are largely lost. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Eastern Roman Empire; events in the Western Empire, Britain, and beyond the imperial frontiers are poorly attested. The execution of Crispus is a case study in how ancient sources can be unreliable: multiple conflicting accounts survive, and modern historians debate whether Constantine acted justly or was manipulated by Fausta.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Nicaea established the Nicene Creed, which was reaffirmed and refined at the First Council of Constantinople in 381 CE
Nicene orthodoxy became the foundation for subsequent ecumenical councils, including the Council of Chalcedon in 451 CE
The execution of Crispus destabilized the succession and led to the execution of Fausta
Continued construction of Constantinople culminated in its formal dedication in 330 CE
Constantine's sole rule after defeating Licinius enabled him to convene the ecumenical council
